3A_Bibliographic.citation: Zhao, Y., Han, X., Bagot, K. S., Tapert, S. F., Potenza, M. N., & Paulus, M. P. (2025). Examining measurement discrepancies in adolescent screen media activity with insights from the ABCD study. npj Mental Health Research, 4, 15.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Release 5.1 data included 4754 participants at Year-4 follow-up. Of these, 1483 participants (753 girls) agreed to install the EARS smartphone app, which passively collected app usage data for 3 weeks. However, EARS-logged data were only available for 495 Android users, as Apple blocked app-scrapping programs at data-collection time. Therefore, analyses focused on 495 participants with the EARS-logged data.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Social media use measured via both self-report (ABCD Screen Time Questionnaire) and objective passive sensing (EARS app-logged usage time)
3C_Exposure.exposure_instrument: ABCD Youth Screen Time Questionnaire and EARS (Effortless Assessment of Risk States) smartphone app
3C_Exposure.modalities: total screen time, social media, gaming
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous hours/day (self-report typical day and EARS-logged average daily app usage)
3C_Exposure.time_frame: Typical day (self-report past 12 months and post-sensing 3 weeks) and 3-week EARS passive sensing period
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Subjective SMA was assessed by youth self-reported time involved in screen activities (TV/video, video games, text, social media, etc.) on a typical day over the past 12 months from the ABCD Screen Time Questionnaire. Post-sensing survey data about SMA during the three-week sensing period were also analyzed. Analyses focused on total daily hours and hours spent on social media and video gaming. Objective usage time was extracted from the raw EARS data. Total EARS-logged time was calculated as the sum of the foreground app usage times across all apps averaged over days with at least one upload during the tracking period.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Behavioral problems (CBCL internalizing and externalizing symptoms) and problematic technology use (SMAQ, VGAQ)
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL) internalizing and externalizing subscales; Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ); Videogame Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ)
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Youth behavioral problems (n = 492) were assessed using the parent-report child behavior checklist subscales in the domains of internalizing problems (i.e., withdrawn/depressed, anxiety/depressed, somatic complaints) and externalizing problems (i.e., rule-breaking behavior, aggressive behavior). Higher scores reflect more behavioral problems.
3D_Outcome.notes: This paper is primarily a measurement study comparing self-report vs objective screen time, with secondary analyses relating both to behavioral outcomes.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Linear mixed models with family as a random effect; also logistic regression for reporting bias analysis
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, sex, race/ethnicity, family income, parental education, parental marital status
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Pearson correlations and paired two-sample t tests were conducted to assess the strength and significance of associations between self-reported and objectively measured app usage times. Logistic regression models were used to evaluate the relationship between reporting bias in SMA and cognitive performance. Linear mixed models, with family as a random effect, were used to examine associations between SMA (both subjective and objective) and behavioral problems. Standardized estimates were reported to facilitate cross-measure comparisons.

3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: both EARS (beta = 0.06, 95% CI: 0.01-0.11) and self-report during the sensing period (beta = 0.08, 95% CI: 0.03-0.14) and prior 12 months (beta = 0.15, 95% CI: 0.10-0.20)
self-reported social media use over the prior 12 months was also related to VGAQ (beta = 0.05, 95% CI: 0.01-0.10)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Under-reporting = self-report at least 30 min less than EARS-logged

3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: The paper does not use significance language in the abstract but reports exact p-values and CIs. The sample is relatively small (n=495) for an ABCD paper. The standardized betas are very small (0.05-0.15) but effect size magnitude is never explicitly discussed.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Recommendations to parents regarding passive monitoring of youth SMA, sleep, exercise, and possibly other domains could support personalized discussions on healthy versus risky behaviors and inform health recommendations from relevant academic/clinical groups and policymakers.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion focuses primarily on methodological implications, particularly the discrepancies between self-reported and passively sensed screen time, and the role of cognitive factors (picture vocabulary) in reporting accuracy. The paper highlights that both objective and subjective measures relate to externalizing problems but only self-report relates to internalizing problems, suggesting each measurement approach captures different aspects of screen use. The tone is measured and methodologically oriented rather than alarmist.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: This paper is primarily a measurement/methods paper rather than a traditional exposure-outcome study. The primary focus is on discrepancies between self-report and EARS-logged screen time rather than associations with health outcomes, making coding some fields (e.g., primary outcome) a judgment call.
